what_this_means.stem: Priya gets blindsided by harsh feedback in a meeting. Rather than snapping at the next person she talks to, she takes a walk, names what stung about it, and keeps the one useful point. What pattern is this?

options.text: Conduit — passing the feedback through unchanged
Absorber — holding onto the sting indefinitely
Transmuter — filtering the deprivation and keeping only what's useful
Extractor — drawing value from the person who criticized her

what_this_means.explanation: Transmutation Capacity is the ability to filter deprivation (harsh feedback) and let go of what isn't useful, rather than passing it on or holding onto all of it. Priya is actively filtering.

what_this_means.stem: The four archetypes measured here are Transmuter, Magnifier, Absorber, and Extractor, with Conduit as the neutral baseline. Which one is the default, morally neutral starting point most people operate from?

options.text: Transmuter — transmutation is the default for most people
Magnifier — amplifying is what most people do first
Conduit — passing things through largely unchanged, the common starting point
Extractor — most people extract value from situations

what_this_means.explanation: Conduit is the neutral baseline: passing deprivation and fulfillment through roughly unchanged. It's not a flaw — it's simply where most people start before developing more active filtering or amplifying capacity.

your_score.stem: Your profile shows: Deprivation Filtering 4/10, Fulfillment Emission 8/10, Amplification Awareness 6/10, Absorption Patterns 3/10 (lower is better here). What does this combination suggest?

options.text: You struggle across the board and should focus everywhere at once
You're strong at sharing good experiences outward but have more capacity to build in processing tough ones without carrying them
Your scores are meaningless without comparing to other people
This means you are an Extractor

your_score.explanation: Reading sub-dimensions together, not in isolation, is the point: high Fulfillment Emission and low Absorption Patterns show real strength in sharing the good — while lower Deprivation Filtering points to room to grow in processing hard moments before they pass through.

your_score.stem: Two people score the same overall Transmutation Capacity total, but User A's four sub-scores are all close together, while User B has one very high sub-score and one very low one. What's the practical difference?

options.text: There is no practical difference — the total is what matters
User B likely has an inconsistent pattern — strong in one specific way but with a clear gap elsewhere — while User A's capacity is more evenly developed
User A is automatically the Transmuter archetype
User B's low sub-score cancels out and should be ignored

your_score.explanation: The overall total can look identical while the underlying pattern is very different. An even profile (User A) suggests balanced development; an uneven one (User B) points to a specific, addressable gap alongside a real strength.

daily_effects.stem: Someone with strong Deprivation Filtering but low Fulfillment Emission leads a team. Predict how this shows up day to day.

options.text: They shield the team well from setbacks but rarely pass along praise or good news, so wins can feel muted
They amplify every piece of bad news to the team
They are unaffected by anything that happens at work
They extract credit from every team success

daily_effects.explanation: Filtering deprivation well (absorbing setbacks so the team doesn't have to) is a real strength. Without matching Fulfillment Emission, though, good news and recognition may not flow back out as readily — a specific, learnable gap, not a character flaw.

daily_effects.stem: Marcus tutors kids in an under-resourced school every week. He goes home each time replaying the unfairness he witnessed, and it follows him into the next day. He keeps going because it matters to him. What's happening?

options.text: Transmuter — he is transforming the deprivation he witnesses
Absorber — he's taking in the deprivation around him without yet having a way to filter or release it, which is leading to carryover fatigue
Magnifier — he is amplifying the unfairness to others
Conduit — the experience passes through him unaffected

daily_effects.explanation: This is Absorption: taking in real deprivation (systemic unfairness) without a developed way to filter it. The result is carryover fatigue, not a personal failing. Building filtering capacity — not caring less — is what changes the pattern.
daily_effects.reflection_prompt: Where in your own life do you notice something similar — caring so much that the weight of it follows you home? What's one small way you could process it rather than just carry it?

strengths_gaps.stem: Your profile shows Transmutation Capacity as your lowest-scoring dimension, while Emotional Awareness and Reflective Functioning are both strong. What does this combination suggest?

options.text: The lower score means the other scores must be wrong
You have the awareness to notice flows of deprivation and fulfillment clearly — the growth edge is building the capacity to actively transform them, not seeing them
You should stop working on Transmutation Capacity since it's your weakest area
This combination is impossible

strengths_gaps.explanation: This is a common and workable pattern: strong perception (you see the flows) with developing transformation capacity (you're still building how to act on what you see). It's untapped capacity, not a deficit.

strengths_gaps.stem: Someone scores very consistently across all four Transmutation Capacity sub-dimensions — no sharp peaks or dips. What does this suggest about where to focus?

options.text: They have no strengths worth building on
Their growth may come less from fixing one specific sub-dimension and more from deepening the overall capacity through practice
Consistent scores always indicate the Conduit archetype
There's nothing meaningful to learn from an even profile

strengths_gaps.explanation: An even profile doesn't point to one glaring gap to patch — it suggests the more useful move is deepening overall capacity through consistent practice, rather than triaging a single weak spot.

external_interaction.stem: In a household where one parent tends to filter stress well and the other tends to amplify it, how does household stress typically move?

options.text: Stress disappears entirely because one parent filters well
Stress entering the household is inconsistently processed — sometimes absorbed and softened, sometimes amplified — depending on which parent it passes through
Household stress has nothing to do with either parent's patterns
The amplifying parent's pattern has no effect on the household

external_interaction.explanation: In shared systems like households, one person's filtering doesn't cancel out another's amplifying — stress moving through the system is shaped by whichever pattern it encounters, which is why understanding both patterns helps the whole household.

external_interaction.stem: A community organizer wants to shift a neighborhood from a pattern where most residents absorb hardship silently toward one where more people actively filter and share resources. What's a realistic first step?

options.text: Tell people to stop feeling the hardship
Create small, low-stakes spaces where people can name what they're carrying and see it met with support rather than judgment, building filtering capacity gradually
Wait for the pattern to change on its own
Focus only on residents who already filter well

external_interaction.explanation: Systemic patterns shift through repeated, low-stakes practice in a supportive context — not by asking people to stop feeling hardship. Building collective filtering capacity is gradual, structural work.

what_this_means.stem: Jordan feels their stomach tighten during a tense conversation and can name it immediately: 'I'm anxious about this.' What does this demonstrate?

options.text: Emotional suppression
Emotional clarity — accurately identifying the feeling as it happens
Emotional avoidance
A lack of emotional awareness

what_this_means.explanation: Emotional Awareness & Regulation includes clarity: being able to name what you're feeling specifically, in the moment, rather than a vague sense of 'off.'

what_this_means.stem: This dimension covers noticing feelings as they arise, naming them clearly, and being able to calm yourself when they become intense. Which of these is NOT part of what it measures?

options.text: Noticing an emotion as it starts
Naming an emotion specifically
Calming down after intense emotion
Predicting the stock market

what_this_means.explanation: The dimension covers attention to feelings, clarity about what they are, and the capacity to regulate — bring yourself back to steadier ground. It has nothing to do with unrelated skills like financial prediction.

your_score.stem: Your score suggests you often notice you're upset only after a while — but once you do notice, you're highly skilled at calming down. What does this pattern point to?

options.text: You have no emotional skills at all
Attention (catching the feeling early) is the growth edge, while regulation (settling once you notice) is already a real strength
Your regulation skill is fake
This pattern is impossible to have

your_score.explanation: Attention and regulation are separate skills that can develop unevenly. Noticing feelings earlier is a learnable skill, and it builds on the calming ability you already have.

your_score.stem: Two people score similarly overall, but one struggles mainly with naming what they feel (clarity) while the other struggles mainly with calming down once upset (regulation). How should they each focus their practice?

options.text: Both should do the exact same exercises
The first benefits from practices that build a richer emotional vocabulary; the second benefits more from calming techniques like breathing or pausing before responding
Neither can improve
Only the second person's gap is real

your_score.explanation: Different sub-skills call for different practices — vocabulary-building for clarity gaps, and calming techniques for regulation gaps. Matching the practice to the specific gap is more effective than a one-size-fits-all approach.

daily_effects.stem: Someone with low emotional clarity but high emotional attention notices they're 'off' throughout the day but can't say why. Predict a common daily effect.

options.text: They feel completely at peace all day
They may feel a persistent, hard-to-place unease that's tiring to carry without being able to address it directly
They become experts at managing their emotions
This has no effect on their day

daily_effects.explanation: Noticing something is wrong without being able to name it specifically is its own kind of tiring — you can't address what you can't identify. Building clarity turns vague unease into something workable.

daily_effects.stem: Sam snaps at a coworker over something minor, then realizes afterward they'd actually been building frustration all morning without noticing. What does this suggest?

options.text: Sam has excellent emotional attention
A gap in early emotional attention let frustration build unnoticed until it surfaced as a snap reaction
Sam's coworker caused the entire problem
This is unrelated to emotional awareness

daily_effects.explanation: When early signals go unnoticed, feelings can build until they surface more intensely than the triggering moment warrants. Catching the frustration earlier — not suppressing it — is the growth edge here.
daily_effects.reflection_prompt: Think of a recent moment you reacted more strongly than the situation seemed to call for. Looking back, was there an earlier signal you might have caught?

strengths_gaps.stem: Your profile shows strong emotional clarity but a developing capacity for regulation under pressure. What's a fair way to frame this?

options.text: You are bad at emotions
You clearly see what you're feeling — the next step is building tools to steady yourself once you do, which is a learnable skill, not a fixed trait
Clarity without regulation is worthless
There's nothing to work on

strengths_gaps.explanation: Seeing clearly and staying steady are related but distinct capacities. Having one well-developed already gives you a head start on building the other — it's an addition, not evidence of failure.

strengths_gaps.stem: Someone with strong regulation but developing attention often calms down quickly but rarely notices patterns in what upsets them. What would help them grow?

options.text: Nothing — regulation alone is enough
Practices that build noticing, like briefly checking in with their body or mood at set points in the day, so patterns become visible over time
They should stop regulating so well
This combination cannot exist

strengths_gaps.explanation: Regulation without attention can mean recurring triggers go unexamined. Simple noticing practices build the missing piece without undoing the regulation strength already there.

external_interaction.stem: In a workplace where most people have low emotional clarity, how do conflicts typically get resolved?

options.text: Quickly and cleanly every time
Slowly and messily, since people struggle to name what's actually bothering them, so surface issues get addressed while the real one lingers
Conflicts never arise in such workplaces
Emotional clarity has no bearing on workplace conflict

external_interaction.explanation: When people can't name what they're actually feeling, conflict resolution tends to address symptoms rather than causes. Teams that build shared emotional vocabulary often resolve things faster.

external_interaction.stem: A manager wants to build a team culture where people notice and name emotional undercurrents earlier, before they escalate. What's a realistic first step?

options.text: Ban all displays of emotion at work
Model naming their own reactions in low-stakes moments ('I'm noticing I'm a bit frustrated by this delay') so it becomes normal to do the same
Require everyone to take a personality test
Ignore the issue until someone quits

external_interaction.explanation: Modeling is one of the most effective ways to shift group norms — when a leader names their own feelings plainly and without drama, it gives others permission to do the same.

what_this_means.stem: After an argument, Leah pauses and wonders, 'What might have been going on for him that made him react that way?' What is she demonstrating?

options.text: Avoidance
Reflective functioning — considering the mental state behind someone's behavior
Emotional suppression
Judgment

what_this_means.explanation: Reflective Functioning is the capacity to consider that behavior is driven by underlying thoughts, feelings, and intentions — your own and other people's — rather than taking actions at face value.

what_this_means.stem: This dimension is about making sense of behavior — yours and others' — in terms of underlying mental states. Which of these best captures it?

options.text: Predicting the weather
Assuming everyone thinks exactly like you do
Recognizing that people (including you) act based on beliefs, feelings, and intentions that aren't always visible on the surface
Memorizing facts about psychology

what_this_means.explanation: The core skill is holding in mind that there's an inner world — beliefs, feelings, intentions — behind visible behavior, and being curious about it rather than assuming you already know.

your_score.stem: Your score suggests you're often puzzled by why people do what they do, and your own reactions sometimes surprise you too. What does this suggest?

options.text: You are incapable of understanding people
Building a habit of pausing to ask 'what might be going on underneath this' — for others and for yourself — is a concrete, learnable next step
This score means nothing
You should stop trying to understand others

your_score.explanation: Being puzzled by behavior is a starting point, not a verdict. The specific, practiced habit of pausing to consider underlying mental states is exactly what builds this capacity over time.

your_score.stem: One person scores high on understanding their OWN mental states but lower on reading others'; another is the reverse. How should each approach practice?

options.text: Identically — there's no difference
The first might practice more perspective-taking exercises with others; the second might practice noticing and naming their own internal reactions more
Neither can improve since the skill is fixed
Only self-understanding matters

your_score.explanation: Reflective functioning has both self- and other-directed components, and they can develop at different rates. Practice aimed at the specific gap is more useful than generic advice.

daily_effects.stem: A coworker is unusually short with someone who has lower reflective functioning. Predict their likely first interpretation.

options.text: They'll wonder what might be going on for the coworker underneath the shortness
They'll likely take it at face value as personal rudeness, without considering other explanations
They'll assume the coworker is having a great day
They won't notice the interaction at all

daily_effects.explanation: Without the habit of considering underlying mental states, behavior tends to get read literally — 'they were rude' rather than 'something might be going on for them.' This isn't a character flaw; it's a specific, buildable skill.

daily_effects.stem: After being interrupted in a meeting, Dana feels a flash of anger she doesn't examine, and later realizes it was really about feeling unheard, not the interruption itself. What happened?

options.text: Dana has no emotions
In the moment, Dana's reflective functioning about her own reaction was limited — she later did the reflective work that revealed the deeper feeling underneath the surface anger
The interruption was objectively wrong
This is unrelated to reflective functioning

daily_effects.explanation: The delayed insight — realizing the real feeling underneath the initial reaction — is reflective functioning in action, just after the fact rather than in the moment. Practicing it in real time is the next step.
daily_effects.reflection_prompt: Think of a time your first reaction to something later turned out to be about something deeper. What helped you notice the deeper layer?

strengths_gaps.stem: Your profile shows strong reflective functioning about others but less about your own internal states. How should this be framed?

options.text: You are self-absorbed
You've built real skill in understanding other people's inner worlds — turning that same curiosity inward is untapped capacity, not a deficiency
Understanding others is a waste if you can't understand yourself
This combination is not possible

strengths_gaps.explanation: The skill of considering others' mental states can exist alongside a less-developed habit of examining your own — and the good news is the underlying capacity (curiosity about inner states) already exists; it just needs to be pointed inward too.

strengths_gaps.stem: Someone scores low on reflective functioning across the board but is highly motivated to grow this area. What would a realistic starting practice look like?

options.text: Immediately mastering complex psychological theory
A simple daily habit: after a strong reaction (yours or someone else's), pausing to ask 'what might really be going on here?'
Giving up since the skill can't be built
Avoiding all situations that require this skill

strengths_gaps.explanation: Reflective functioning builds through small, repeated practice — a simple pause-and-wonder habit — much more reliably than through trying to master theory all at once.

external_interaction.stem: In a team where reflective functioning is generally low, how do misunderstandings typically play out?

options.text: They resolve themselves instantly
They tend to escalate, since behavior gets read at face value and assumed intentions go unchecked rather than questioned
They never happen
Reflective functioning has no bearing on team dynamics

external_interaction.explanation: Low reflective functioning across a group means assumed intentions ('they must not care') go unchallenged, which tends to escalate rather than resolve friction.

external_interaction.stem: A team lead wants to build a norm of considering intentions before reacting to friction. What's a workable first step?

options.text: Punish anyone who reacts without reflecting
Introduce a simple team habit — like asking 'what might be going on for them?' out loud before jumping to conclusions in a tense moment
Assume the team will figure it out alone
Avoid all team conflict entirely

external_interaction.explanation: Making the reflective pause an explicit, shared habit — rather than an invisible individual skill — is one of the most practical ways to build it at the team level.

what_this_means.stem: After making a mistake at work, Theo tells himself, 'That was rough, and mistakes happen to everyone learning something new,' rather than harshly criticizing himself. What is this?

options.text: Self-pity
Self-compassion — treating yourself with the same kindness you'd offer a friend
Avoiding responsibility
Arrogance

what_this_means.explanation: Self-Compassion means responding to your own struggles or mistakes with the same warmth and understanding you'd naturally offer someone else — not excusing the mistake, but not attacking yourself over it either.

what_this_means.stem: This dimension has three parts: being kind to yourself in hard moments, recognizing that struggle is part of being human (not uniquely yours), and holding painful feelings without being swept away by them. Which is NOT one of the three?

options.text: Self-kindness
Common humanity
Balanced mindfulness of pain
Constant positive thinking

what_this_means.explanation: Self-Compassion isn't about forcing positivity — it's kindness toward yourself, recognizing shared human struggle, and holding difficult feelings in balance rather than either suppressing or being overwhelmed by them.

your_score.stem: Your score suggests you're quick to extend understanding to friends who struggle, but much harsher with yourself in the same situation. What does this pattern point to?

options.text: You don't actually care about your friends
The capacity for compassion is clearly there — the growth edge is directing some of that same warmth toward yourself
This means you should stop being kind to friends too
This pattern is unusual and concerning

your_score.explanation: This is one of the most common self-compassion patterns — the skill of compassion is present and working, it's just aimed outward more than inward. That's an encouraging starting point, not a flaw.

your_score.stem: One person scores low mainly on self-kindness (they're harsh with themselves) while another scores low mainly on common humanity (they feel uniquely flawed when struggling). How should their practice differ?

options.text: Identically — self-compassion is one single skill
The first benefits from practicing warmer self-talk in tough moments; the second benefits from noticing how universal their specific struggle actually is
Neither can be helped
Only the first person has a real issue

your_score.explanation: Self-compassion has distinct components that can be uneven. Matching practice to the specific gap — warmer self-talk versus recognizing shared humanity — is more effective than generic advice.

daily_effects.stem: Someone with low self-compassion misses a deadline. Predict a likely internal response, compared to someone with higher self-compassion in the same situation.

options.text: Both will respond identically
The first may spiral into harsh self-criticism that makes it harder to problem-solve; the second is more likely to acknowledge the miss, learn from it, and move forward
The first will handle it better
Self-compassion has no bearing on this situation

daily_effects.explanation: Harsh self-criticism tends to consume energy that could go toward actually fixing the problem. Self-compassion isn't about lowering standards — it's about staying functional enough to meet them.

daily_effects.stem: After a difficult breakup, Mei tells herself she's the only person who's ever struggled this much and feels isolated in her pain, even though friends have been through similar things. What's missing?

options.text: Self-kindness
Common humanity — the recognition that struggle like this is part of the shared human experience, not evidence that something is uniquely wrong with her
Nothing is missing; this is a healthy response
Balanced mindfulness

daily_effects.explanation: Feeling isolated in pain, as if you're the only one who's ever struggled this way, is exactly what the common-humanity component addresses — remembering that this kind of hurt connects you to others rather than separating you.
daily_effects.reflection_prompt: Think of a hard time you felt alone in your struggle. Can you think of someone else who's been through something similar? How might that change how you're holding the experience?

strengths_gaps.stem: Your profile shows strong common humanity (you know struggle is universal) but developing self-kindness (your self-talk is still often harsh). What's a fair way to describe this?

options.text: You are a hypocrite
You have a genuinely useful perspective on shared struggle already — building warmer self-talk is the next, learnable layer on top of that
This combination means the whole assessment is wrong
There's nothing to build here

strengths_gaps.explanation: Common humanity and self-kindness are related but separate skills. Having a strong perspective on shared human struggle gives you a real foundation to build warmer self-talk on top of.

strengths_gaps.stem: Someone scores low across all three self-compassion components but wants to start somewhere. What's a realistic, small first practice?

options.text: Immediately forgiving themselves for everything with no reflection
Noticing one moment of self-criticism a day and asking, 'What would I say to a friend in this exact situation?'
Giving up because it can't be learned
Avoiding all situations where mistakes are possible

strengths_gaps.explanation: The friend-comparison exercise is a well-supported starting practice: it's concrete, low-effort, and directly builds the habit of extending the same warmth to yourself that you already extend to others.

external_interaction.stem: In a workplace culture that treats every mistake as a personal failing, how does this typically affect employees' self-compassion over time?

options.text: It has no effect — self-compassion is entirely internal
It tends to erode self-compassion, since harsh external framing of mistakes gets internalized as harsh self-framing
It always increases self-compassion
Employees become immune to the culture

external_interaction.explanation: Self-compassion doesn't develop in a vacuum — environments that treat mistakes punitively tend to shape how people talk to themselves, too. This is why workplace and family culture matter for this dimension.

external_interaction.stem: A parent wants to help their child develop self-compassion rather than harsh self-criticism after mistakes. What's a realistic approach?

options.text: Never let the child experience any failure
Model self-compassionate self-talk out loud when the parent makes their own mistakes, so the child sees an alternative to self-attack
Punish the child for being upset about mistakes
Tell the child mistakes don't matter at all

external_interaction.explanation: Children often learn self-talk patterns by watching adults model them. A parent narrating their own self-compassionate response to a mistake is one of the most effective, low-cost ways to teach the skill.

what_this_means.stem: Before responding to a frustrating email, Kai imagines what it might feel like to be the sender, dealing with whatever pressures they might be under. What is this?

options.text: Overthinking
Perspective-taking — imagining another person's viewpoint before reacting
Avoidance
People-pleasing

what_this_means.explanation: Relational Awareness & Compassion includes perspective-taking: pausing to genuinely consider another person's point of view, which often changes how you choose to respond.

what_this_means.stem: This dimension combines noticing others' distress, genuinely wanting to help, and being able to see situations from another person's point of view. Which is NOT part of it?

options.text: Noticing when someone is struggling
Wanting to help when you notice distress
Seeing a situation from another's viewpoint
Winning every argument

what_this_means.explanation: This dimension is about compassion and perspective-taking in relationships — noticing distress, caring, and seeing others' viewpoints — not about competitive skills like winning arguments.

your_score.stem: Your score suggests you often notice when someone nearby is struggling but aren't always sure what to do about it. What does this point to?

options.text: You don't actually care about others
The noticing and caring — the harder parts to build — are already there; the next step is developing a small repertoire of simple responses ('are you doing okay?')
Noticing distress is a waste of time
This pattern means you should stop noticing

your_score.explanation: Noticing distress and genuinely wanting to help are the foundation of this dimension — and you already have them. Building a few go-to responses is a much smaller step than developing the underlying care from scratch.

your_score.stem: One person scores high on perspective-taking but lower on noticing distress in the first place; another is the reverse. How should each practice differently?

options.text: Identically — it's one skill

The first might practice scanning for subtle cues (tone, body language) that someone's struggling; the second already sees distress but might practice actively imagining the other person's specific situation
Neither gap matters
Only noticing distress is worth building

your_score.explanation: Noticing distress and taking perspective are related but distinguishable skills. Practicing the specific gap — attentiveness to cues versus imaginative perspective-taking — is more useful than a blanket approach.

daily_effects.stem: A friend is unusually quiet at dinner. Predict how someone with strong relational awareness is likely to respond, compared to someone with a developing capacity in this area.

options.text: Both will respond the same way
The first is more likely to gently check in ('you seem quiet tonight — everything okay?'); the second may not consciously register the shift at all
The second will definitely respond better
This has nothing to do with relational awareness

daily_effects.explanation: Picking up on subtle shifts in someone's demeanor and responding with a gentle check-in is exactly what this dimension supports — and it's a skill that sharpens with attention and practice.

daily_effects.stem: During a disagreement, Ravi pauses and says, 'I can see why you'd be upset about that from where you're standing,' even though he still disagrees with the conclusion. What is he doing?

options.text: Giving in to avoid conflict
Perspective-taking — validating the other person's viewpoint as understandable without necessarily agreeing with it
Being dishonest about his own opinion
This is unrelated to relational awareness

daily_effects.explanation: Perspective-taking doesn't require agreement — it means genuinely seeing why a viewpoint makes sense from where the other person stands, which often lowers tension even amid real disagreement.
daily_effects.reflection_prompt: Think of a recent disagreement. Could you name, out loud, why the other person's view made sense from where they were standing — even if you still disagreed?

strengths_gaps.stem: Your profile shows strong perspective-taking but developing skill in translating that into visible action when someone's struggling. How should this be framed?

options.text: You are all talk, no action
You already do the harder cognitive work of seeing others clearly — the growth edge is building simple, low-stakes ways to act on what you see
Perspective-taking without action is worthless
There's nothing to build on here

strengths_gaps.explanation: Seeing clearly and acting on it are related but separate skills, and the seeing is often the harder one to build. Having it already gives you a strong base to add small, concrete actions on top of.

strengths_gaps.stem: Someone scores low across this whole dimension but genuinely wants to grow. What's a realistic starting practice?

options.text: Immediately becoming a therapist-level listener
A simple daily habit: once a day, pause and silently guess what someone nearby might be feeling, without needing to act on it yet
Avoiding all social interaction to reduce risk
Assuming the skill can't be developed

strengths_gaps.explanation: Low-stakes, repeated noticing practice (without pressure to immediately act) builds the underlying muscle gradually and safely, before adding the action component.

external_interaction.stem: On a team where relational awareness is generally low, how do interpersonal frictions typically get handled?

options.text: They resolve quickly through mutual understanding
They tend to fester, since subtle signs of tension go unnoticed until they surface as a larger conflict
They never arise
Relational awareness has no bearing on team friction

external_interaction.explanation: Low collective relational awareness means small signs of friction — a clipped tone, a withdrawn teammate — often go unaddressed until they compound into bigger issues.

external_interaction.stem: A manager wants to build a team culture where people notice and respond to each other's struggles earlier. What's a workable first step?

options.text: Mandate that everyone share personal problems publicly
Start meetings with a brief, low-pressure check-in question, normalizing the practice of noticing how people are actually doing
Ignore interpersonal dynamics entirely and focus only on output
Assign a single person to monitor everyone's mood

external_interaction.explanation: Structured, low-pressure check-ins are a practical way to build collective relational awareness without requiring anyone to over-share — they just make noticing and naming normal.

what_this_means.stem: While making a decision, Noor pauses and asks herself, 'Wait, am I about to do this because it's actually the best option, or just because it's familiar?' What is she doing?

options.text: Overanalyzing everything
Meta-cognition — reflecting on her own thought process, not just the decision itself
Avoiding the decision
Second-guessing without reason

what_this_means.explanation: Meta-Cognitive Awareness is thinking about your own thinking — examining the reasoning process itself, not just the conclusion it produces.

what_this_means.stem: This dimension covers noticing your own thought patterns, catching biases in your reasoning, and comparing how you think about something now versus how you used to think about it. Which is NOT part of it?

options.text: Noticing recurring thought patterns
Catching a bias in your own reasoning
Comparing your current thinking to your past thinking on an issue
Being naturally right about everything

what_this_means.explanation: Meta-cognition is about the process of examining your own thinking — not about always being correct. In fact, effective meta-cognition often means catching yourself being wrong.

your_score.stem: Your score suggests you regularly notice patterns in your own thinking but rarely catch specific biases as they happen. What does this suggest?

options.text: You have no meta-cognitive ability at all
The broader noticing habit is well developed; the more specific skill of catching individual biases in the moment is the next layer to build
Noticing patterns is a waste of effort
This combination cannot exist

your_score.explanation: General self-monitoring and specific bias-catching are related but distinguishable skills — one being strong gives you a solid base to build the more granular skill on top of.

your_score.stem: One person's meta-cognition mainly shows up as revisiting old conclusions when new evidence appears; another mainly shows up as noticing in-the-moment reasoning errors. How should their growth differ?

options.text: Identically — it's one skill
The first is already strong at long-term updating and might practice more real-time noticing; the second is strong in-the-moment and might practice periodically revisiting older beliefs
Neither type is meaningful
Only real-time noticing counts as meta-cognition

your_score.explanation: Meta-cognition shows up on different timescales — in-the-moment catches and longer-term belief revision. Recognizing which one is already strong helps target practice on the other.

daily_effects.stem: Someone with strong meta-cognitive awareness receives information that contradicts a belief they've held for years. Predict their likely response compared to someone with less developed meta-cognition.

options.text: Both will respond identically
The first is more likely to pause and genuinely weigh the new evidence against the old belief; the second may dismiss it quickly to avoid the discomfort of reconsidering
The second will update their belief faster
This has nothing to do with meta-cognition

daily_effects.explanation: Being willing to examine and potentially revise your own thinking — rather than defending it reflexively — is a hallmark of strong meta-cognitive awareness, and it's a skill that improves with practice.

daily_effects.stem: Aiden catches himself thinking, 'This new employee is probably going to be difficult,' and pauses to ask whether that's based on real evidence or just an early, unverified impression. What is happening?

options.text: Aiden is being paranoid
Aiden is catching a potential bias in real time — noticing the thought and questioning its basis rather than accepting it automatically
This is unrelated to meta-cognition
Aiden should trust his first impression without question

daily_effects.explanation: Catching an assumption as it forms and questioning its evidence base — rather than acting on it automatically — is meta-cognition in action, and it's a skill that gets easier with repetition.
daily_effects.reflection_prompt: Think of a recent first impression you formed about someone. Have you checked whether it's held up, or is it still just an unverified guess?

strengths_gaps.stem: Your profile shows strong meta-cognition about your own beliefs but less about your emotional reactions in the moment. How should this be framed?

options.text: You are only capable of thinking, not feeling
You've built real skill in examining your reasoning — extending that same reflective habit to emotional reactions in real time is the natural next layer, not a separate deficiency
This combination invalidates the whole assessment
There's nothing more to build

strengths_gaps.explanation: Meta-cognition applied to beliefs and meta-cognition applied to emotions in the moment draw on the same underlying skill, applied to different material — one being strong is a genuine asset for building the other.

strengths_gaps.stem: Someone scores low on meta-cognition overall but wants a realistic starting practice. What's a reasonable first step?

options.text: Immediately mastering formal logic and statistics
A brief nightly habit: 'What's one thing I thought today that I'd think differently about now?'
Assuming the skill can't be built
Avoiding all situations that require reflection

strengths_gaps.explanation: A short, repeatable end-of-day reflection is a well-supported way to build meta-cognitive habit gradually, without requiring formal training.

external_interaction.stem: In an organization where leadership rarely reflects on or revises its own past decisions, what's a likely effect over time?

options.text: Decision quality improves automatically
Mistakes tend to repeat, since the pattern-recognition step (noticing what went wrong and why) is skipped
This has no bearing on decision quality
Leadership becomes more adaptable without trying

external_interaction.explanation: Meta-cognition at an organizational level — reviewing why past decisions succeeded or failed — is what prevents the same mistakes from recurring. Its absence tends to produce repeated patterns.

external_interaction.stem: A team lead wants to build a culture of examining the team's own reasoning after projects, not just the outcomes. What's a workable first step?

options.text: Only discuss whether the project succeeded or failed
Add a brief 'how did we think about this' retrospective question alongside the usual 'what happened' review
Blame individuals for any errors in judgment
Skip reviewing past projects entirely

external_interaction.explanation: Separating 'what happened' from 'how did we reason about it' surfaces the thinking patterns that led to outcomes, which is the specific thing meta-cognition targets.

what_this_means.stem: While eating lunch, Ana notices her mind has wandered to tomorrow's meeting for the third time, and gently brings her attention back to her food without frustration. What is this?

options.text: Failing at mindfulness
Mindful presence — noticing the mind has wandered and returning attention without self-criticism
Multitasking successfully
Losing focus permanently

what_this_means.explanation: Mindful Presence isn't about never getting distracted — it's about noticing when attention has wandered and gently returning it, without turning the moment into self-criticism.

what_this_means.stem: This dimension covers staying attentive to the present moment and not judging your own thoughts and feelings as good or bad. Which best captures the second part?

options.text: Forcing yourself to only have positive thoughts
Noticing a thought or feeling without immediately labeling it as something you shouldn't be having
Ignoring all thoughts and feelings entirely
Analyzing every thought in great detail

what_this_means.explanation: Non-judgment in mindfulness means observing a thought or feeling as simply present, rather than immediately criticizing yourself for having it.

your_score.stem: Your score suggests you notice when your mind wanders fairly quickly, but you're often harsh with yourself when it happens. What does this pattern point to?

options.text: You should stop noticing your mind wander
The noticing skill — often the harder part — is already strong; softening the self-critical reaction when it happens is the more specific next step
Noticing your mind wander is pointless
This pattern cannot exist

your_score.explanation: Noticing wandering attention is the foundational skill, and you already have it. Pairing that noticing with a non-judgmental response — rather than self-criticism — is the more targeted next step.

your_score.stem: One person scores high on present-moment attention but lower on non-judgment; another is the reverse. How should their practice differ?

options.text: Identically — mindfulness is one skill
The first might practice responding to distraction with more warmth rather than criticism; the second already has warmth but might practice noticing distraction sooner
Neither gap is meaningful
Only attention matters, not judgment

your_score.explanation: Attention and non-judgment are distinct components of mindfulness that can develop unevenly — targeting practice at the actual gap is more effective than generic meditation advice.

daily_effects.stem: Someone with low mindful presence eats a meal while scrolling their phone. Predict a common effect, compared to someone eating the same meal with full attention.

options.text: No difference in experience either way
The first is likely to finish without really registering the taste or feeling satisfied; the second is more likely to notice and enjoy the meal itself
The first will enjoy the meal more
This has nothing to do with mindful presence

daily_effects.explanation: Divided attention during ordinary activities like eating tends to reduce the actual experience of them — a common, well-documented effect that mindful presence practices directly address.

daily_effects.stem: During a walk, Priya notices herself planning a conversation in her head, and instead of getting frustrated, she simply notes 'planning' and returns attention to her surroundings. What is she doing?

options.text: Suppressing her thoughts
Practicing mindful presence — noticing the mental activity without judgment and gently returning to the present
Failing to plan properly
This is unrelated to mindfulness

daily_effects.explanation: Labeling the mental activity ('planning') without frustration and returning attention is a textbook mindfulness technique — it works with the mind's tendency to wander rather than fighting it.
daily_effects.reflection_prompt: Next time your mind wanders during a routine activity, try gently naming what it wandered to ('planning,' 'worrying') before returning your attention. What does that feel like compared to getting frustrated with yourself?

strengths_gaps.stem: Your profile shows strong non-judgment (you're gentle with your own wandering mind) but developing present-moment attention (your mind wanders often). How should this be framed?

options.text: You are lazy or unfocused
You've already built the self-compassionate response that makes mindfulness sustainable — building more moment-to-moment attention is the next, learnable layer
Non-judgment without attention is pointless
There's nothing to build here

strengths_gaps.explanation: Non-judgment is actually the harder skill for many people to build, and having it already makes attention practice more sustainable — you won't derail it with self-criticism along the way.

strengths_gaps.stem: Someone scores low across mindful presence overall but wants a realistic starting practice. What's a reasonable first step?

options.text: An hour of silent meditation on day one
Choosing one routine daily activity (like brushing teeth) to do with full attention, gently returning focus whenever it wanders
Assuming the skill can't be built
Avoiding all quiet moments to prevent mind-wandering

strengths_gaps.explanation: Attaching a brief mindfulness practice to an existing routine activity is a well-supported, low-friction way to start building the skill without an overwhelming time commitment.

external_interaction.stem: In a culture that treats constant multitasking as a virtue, how does this typically affect people's capacity for mindful presence over time?

options.text: It has no effect since mindfulness is purely individual
It tends to erode it, since attention is constantly pulled in multiple directions and rarely given space to settle
It strengthens mindful presence
Multitasking and mindfulness are unrelated concepts

external_interaction.explanation: Environments that reward constant task-switching train attention to fragment rather than settle, which works against the skill of mindful presence — context matters, not just individual effort.

external_interaction.stem: A workplace wants to build small moments of collective mindful presence into the day without a major cultural overhaul. What's a realistic step?

options.text: Mandate hour-long daily meditation for all staff
Start meetings with 30 seconds of quiet before diving in, giving people a brief moment to arrive and settle
Ban all breaks to maximize focus
Ignore the idea entirely as impractical

external_interaction.explanation: Small, low-commitment practices integrated into existing routines (like a brief pause before meetings) are far more sustainable at a group level than large mandated interventions.

what_this_means.stem: When a coworker misses another deadline, Tom pauses to consider whether the team's workload structure — not just the coworker's habits — might be contributing. What is he doing?

options.text: Making excuses for poor performance
Systems thinking — considering how a structure or incentive shapes individual behavior, not just judging the behavior in isolation
Avoiding the real issue
Being naive about accountability

what_this_means.explanation: Systemic/Temporal Awareness includes systems thinking: recognizing that behavior often reflects the structures and incentives around a person, not only their individual choices.

what_this_means.stem: This dimension combines seeing how parts of a system affect each other, and thinking about how today's choices ripple into the future. Which best captures the future-oriented half?

options.text: Only caring about the present moment
Weighing how a decision made now will matter later, even when the immediate payoff is smaller
Predicting the future with certainty
Ignoring consequences entirely

what_this_means.explanation: The temporal half of this dimension is about factoring future consequences into present decisions — being willing to accept a smaller near-term outcome for a better long-term one.

your_score.stem: Your score suggests you're strong at seeing how systems and incentives shape behavior, but you tend to focus mostly on immediate outcomes rather than long-term ones. What does this suggest?

options.text: You have no useful skills in this area
Your systems-level thinking is a real asset; extending that same big-picture view to include longer time horizons is the specific next step
Systems thinking without future focus is worthless
This combination is impossible

your_score.explanation: Systems thinking and future orientation are related but distinct — one being strong gives you a real foundation, and the growth edge is applying that same big-picture habit across time as well as across structure.

your_score.stem: One person scores high on future orientation but lower on systems thinking; another is the reverse. How should their practice differ?

options.text: Identically — it's one unified skill
The first might practice asking 'what structures are shaping this behavior?' more often; the second might practice asking 'how will this matter in five years?'
Neither gap is worth addressing
Only future orientation matters

your_score.explanation: The two halves of this dimension respond to different prompts — structural questions versus long-horizon questions — so targeted practice beats a one-size-fits-all approach.

daily_effects.stem: A repeated conflict keeps happening on a team. Predict the likely difference between someone with strong systemic awareness and someone with a developing capacity in this area.

options.text: Both will respond identically
The first is more likely to ask what structural factor (workload, unclear roles) keeps producing the same friction; the second may keep attributing it to individual personalities each time
The second will find the root cause faster
This has no bearing on the conflict

daily_effects.explanation: Recurring conflicts often have a structural root that individual-level explanations miss. Systemic awareness helps spot the pattern behind repeated friction rather than re-litigating it as a personality clash each time.

daily_effects.stem: Facing a choice between a fun purchase today and saving toward a longer-term goal, Yusuf pauses and picks the saving option, even though the fun purchase is more appealing right now. What is happening?

options.text: Yusuf is being overly restrictive with himself
Yusuf is weighing future consequences against present temptation and choosing the option that serves his longer-term interest
This decision has nothing to do with temporal awareness
Yusuf should always prioritize immediate rewards

daily_effects.explanation: Choosing a better long-term outcome over a more appealing immediate one is a direct expression of future orientation — a skill that, like others in this dimension, strengthens with practice.
daily_effects.reflection_prompt: Think of a recent choice between something appealing now and something better later. Which did you choose, and how did you decide?

strengths_gaps.stem: Your profile shows strong future orientation but developing systems thinking. How should this be framed?

options.text: You are shortsighted about everything
You already weigh long-term consequences well — extending that same lens to see structural, not just individual, causes is the next layer to build
Future orientation without systems thinking is meaningless
There's nothing left to develop

strengths_gaps.explanation: Future orientation and systems thinking reinforce each other but can develop separately. Having one strong gives you real groundwork for building the other.

strengths_gaps.stem: Someone scores low across this whole dimension but wants a realistic starting practice. What's a reasonable first step?

options.text: Immediately mastering complex systems theory
Before a decision, briefly asking two questions: 'what structure is shaping this?' and 'how will this look in a year?'
Assuming the skill can't be built
Avoiding all decisions that require this thinking

strengths_gaps.explanation: Two simple, repeatable prompt questions are a practical way to build both halves of this dimension gradually, without requiring formal systems-theory training.

external_interaction.stem: In an organization where leadership consistently prioritizes short-term wins over long-term structural health, what's a likely effect over several years?

options.text: Long-term outcomes improve automatically
Recurring structural problems tend to persist, since short-term fixes don't address underlying causes
This pattern has no long-term effect
The organization becomes more systemically healthy over time

external_interaction.explanation: Consistently trading long-term structural health for short-term wins tends to let underlying problems compound rather than resolve — a pattern systemic/temporal awareness is designed to help interrupt.

external_interaction.stem: A community group wants to shift from treating each local problem as a one-off event toward seeing recurring patterns across generations or systems. What's a workable first step?

options.text: Address each new problem in total isolation from past ones
Start keeping a simple shared record of recurring issues over time, to make patterns across years — not just single incidents — visible to the group
Assume the past has no bearing on current problems
Focus only on immediate, one-time fixes

external_interaction.explanation: Making patterns visible across time — through simple shared tracking — is a practical, low-tech way for a group to start building systemic/temporal awareness together.
